_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Macon County, IL. Decatur — the soybean capital and Staley/ADM agribusiness center — on the flat glacial prairie drained by the Sangamon River. Where the Corn Belt met industrial food processing and Abraham Lincoln made his first political speech.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Till Plains section of the Central Lowland Province. Level to gently rolling Wisconsinan glacial terrain, the flattest landscape in the cluster. The Grand Prairie at its most characteristic — horizon-to-horizon black soil.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 620–680 ft. Imperceptible variation except along the Sangamon River valley.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Extremely flat glacial till plain with the Sangamon River cutting a broad, marshy valley through the center. Lake Decatur (created 1922 by damming the Sangamon) dominates the central landscape. The terrain required extensive tile drainage before cultivation was possible on the wettest areas.

soil_types.description: Deep, dark, poorly drained prairie soil. The dominant soil across the county's flat uplands. World-class productivity once tiled.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Extraordinarily productive for corn and soybeans. Macon County's agricultural wealth is built on this soil. The A.E. Staley company chose Decatur specifically because the surrounding farmland produced surplus corn and soybeans for processing.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Macon County, Illinois. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1973.

soil_types.description: Moderately well-drained dark prairie soil on gentle slopes. Better natural drainage than Drummer.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Productive for corn and soybeans without intensive tiling. The better-drained uplands were farmed first.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Macon County, Illinois (1973).

major_waterways.name: Sangamon River
major_waterways.role: Primary drainage, Lake Decatur source, Lincoln's flatboat route
major_waterways.description: The Sangamon crosses Macon County in a broad valley. Lincoln's family settled along it in 1830. Lake Decatur (1922) dammed the river for municipal water supply, creating a reservoir that became central to Decatur's identity.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Sangamon River brought the Lincoln family to Macon County in 1830 — and a century later, Decatur dammed it to create the lake that watered the factories processing the soybeans the prairie grew.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Tallgrass prairie with timber along Sangamon River and tributary streams
vegetation_eras.description: Virtually treeless upland prairie — the Grand Prairie at its most open. Timber along the Sangamon provided the only building material. The Kickapoo used the river corridor seasonally.
vegetation_eras.source: Bogue, Allan G. From Prairie to Corn Belt. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1963.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Prairie being broken for corn cultivation; timber diminishing rapidly
vegetation_eras.description: The Lincoln family broke prairie near the Sangamon in 1830. By the Civil War, most upland prairie had been converted to farmland. The steel plow and tile drainage completed the transformation by 1880.
vegetation_eras.source: Bogue, From Prairie to Corn Belt (1963).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Corn and soybean fields; industrial Decatur expanding; grain elevators and processing plants dominating the skyline
vegetation_eras.description: Macon County became the epicenter of soybean processing in America. The A.E. Staley Manufacturing Company (1912) and Archer Daniels Midland (relocated headquarters to Decatur, 1969) made Decatur the soybean capital of the world. The landscape was corn and soybeans to the horizon, broken only by grain elevators, railroad tracks, and the growing industrial complex along Lake Decatur.
vegetation_eras.source: Rund, Robert E. A.E. Staley: The Story of a Great Company and the Man Who Founded It. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1958.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: When the Lincoln family crossed the frozen Sangamon into Macon County in March 1830, the prairie was still winter-brown — dead grass flattened by snow, the river bottom choked with bare sycamore and cottonwood. The cold was relentless, the wind unbroken by any obstacle between the family's wagon and the horizon. The ground was frozen solid, the soil beneath it black as coal and sticky as tar when it thawed. The smell was ice, dead grass, and woodsmoke from the few cabins scattered along the timber. The silence was enormous — no town, no church bell, no mill wheel. Just wind and the creak of the wagon and the river running under ice at the bottom of a shallow valley.
sensory_snapshots.source: Power, John Carroll. History of the Early Settlers of Sangamon County, Illinois. Springfield, 1876.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: By July 1925, Decatur smelled like money — the sweet, heavy, slightly rancid smell of soybeans being crushed and processed at the Staley plant on the shore of Lake Decatur. The plant's smokestacks rose above the flat prairie like industrial monuments, visible from ten miles out on the section-line roads. Inside the city, the streets were paved and the streetcar ran; outside, the corn stood eight feet tall in fields that stretched to the vanishing point. The heat was thick and wet, the humidity trapped by the flat terrain, and the lake shimmered with a greenish haze of algae fertilized by agricultural runoff. At the Staley plant, the clatter of machinery mixed with the hiss of steam and the voices of workers who had come from farms, from the South, and from across the Atlantic to process the prairie's harvest.
sensory_snapshots.source: Rund, A.E. Staley (1958). Macon County Historical Society records.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: October in Macon County in 1943 was harvest under wartime pressure — every bushel of corn and soybeans had a destination in the war economy, and the Staley plant ran three shifts processing soybeans into oil for margarine, meal for livestock feed, and industrial products for the military. The fields were golden-brown with drying corn, the mechanical pickers moving through in straight lines that followed the section grid. The air smelled of grain dust, diesel exhaust, and the ever-present soybean processing odor from Decatur. German prisoners of war from Camp Butler worked the harvest on farms short of labor, and their presence in the fields was a surreal reminder that the war touched everything, even the quietest cornfield in Illinois.
sensory_snapshots.source: Bateman, Newton, and Paul Selby, eds. Historical Encyclopedia of Illinois and History of Macon County. Chicago: Munsell Publishing Co., 1903.

landscape_changes.change: Prairie frontier — Lincoln's first Illinois home
landscape_changes.description: The Lincoln family settled along the Sangamon in 1830. Macon County was raw frontier — fewer than 2,000 people in the entire county. Settlement followed the timber lines along the river, avoiding the open prairie. Lincoln split rails, broke prairie, and made his first political speech in Decatur before moving to New Salem and then Springfield.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} arrived in Macon County before 1850, the county was frontier — tallgrass, timber, and a courthouse in a village called Decatur that was barely a crossroads.
landscape_changes.source: Power, History of the Early Settlers (1876).

landscape_changes.description: A.E. Staley opened a corn processing plant in Decatur in 1912 and expanded into soybean processing in the 1920s. By 1940, Decatur was the soybean processing capital of America. ADM's arrival consolidated the city's identity. The Staley plant employed thousands and gave Decatur an industrial economy unlike any other city in the cluster.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The Staley plant transformed Decatur from a county seat into an industrial city — and {ancestor_name} was either growing the soybeans that fed the plant or working inside it.

narrative_hooks: Lincoln split his first rails and made his first political speech in Macon County — by the time {ancestor_name} arrived in {year}, the prairie Lincoln had broken was producing corn and soybeans at a scale the rail-splitter could not have imagined.
The Staley plant in Decatur turned {ancestor_name}'s soybean crop into oil, meal, and starch — and the smell of that processing hung over the city like a second atmosphere.
Lake Decatur did not exist before 1922 — the Sangamon River ran free through the valley where {ancestor_name}'s family may have farmed, and then the dam came and the water covered it.

Macon County was so flat that the grain elevator in {town} was the tallest thing {ancestor_name} could see from the front porch — a man-made mountain on a landscape that nature had made as level as a table.
source_references: Bogue, Allan G. From Prairie to Corn Belt: Farming on the Illinois and Iowa Prairies in the Nineteenth Century. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1963.
Rund, Robert E. A.E. Staley: The Story of a Great Company and the Man Who Founded It. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1958.
USDA Soil Survey of Macon County, Illinois. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1973.
Power, John Carroll. History of the Early Settlers of Sangamon County, Illinois. Springfield, 1876.
Bateman, Newton, and Paul Selby, eds. Historical Encyclopedia of Illinois and History of Macon County. Chicago: Munsell Publishing Co., 1903.
